sentences.text: Mhm.
Mira, I'd like to begin today by having you tell us your name, and spell your last name for me, please.
My name is Mira, M-I-R-A, my last name is Shelub, S-H-E-L-U-B, like in boy.
During the war I was called Mira, M-I-R-A, and Scelubski, S-C-E-L-U-B-S-K-I.
I was born January 13, 1922.
I was born in this Zdzieciol, Z-D-Z-I-E-C-I-O-L, Poland.
Before I start to tell you my story, I would like to convey to you a message.
[BACKROUND NOISE] Never say they it is too late.
Never say that it is the last road.
Never say that you cannot see the light at the end of the tunnel.
Because we, young men and women, as partisans in the forests for the courageous battle against enemy for a better day, for a better tomorrow, for a better future.
I was born, as I said before, in a little town in Poland, Zdzieciol.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a nice family.
We lead a beautiful, quiet, nice life.
I attended an elementary school, a Jewish elementary-- elementary school, but all the subjects were taught in Yiddish, only history, Polish history, geography, was taught in Polish.
My family-- I had my mother, my father, my grandmother, my brother, and my sister.
My father's name was Chaimmichel, C-H-A-I-M-M-I-C-H-E-L.
Raznov, R-A-Z-N-O-V.
My mother's name was Chana Raznov.
My grandmother's name was Henie my sister's name, Sarah, and my brother's name, Moris.
I started this elementary school and I graduated, and then it was time to move on.
My--
My father was a very clever, smart man.
He was the director of the Yiddishe shule, of the Yiddish school in our town.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had a very nice family life.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And-- [SIGHS]
We were a very close family, very close family.
My father spent lots of time with us, teaching us, talking to us, visiting with us.
My mother was also a very intelligent, nice woman.
She spent-- spent lot of time with us.
Helping us to read books, to enjoy the books, to analyze the books.
Helping us certain activities in our little town.
Like giving us dancing lessons, taking us to different places.
It was a nice family life together.
Yes, we did have lots of activities.
We spend lots of time at our Yiddish school in Zdzieciol, where we participated in different-- in shows, in-- in performances, and so on, and so forth.
I had good friends.
We did lots of things together, we were skiing, skating, belonging to a chorus, walking around-- really enjoyed.
But after I graduated from my Yiddish elementary school, it was time to move on.
And my aim was to attend a gymnasium.
We did not have a gymnasium in our town.
A gymnasium means, high school-- equivalent of high school, plus junior college.
But there was a beautiful gymnasium in Vilna.
I could not afford to go to Vilna, so I applied for a scholarship, and I won a scholarship.
And I cannot tell you the joy, the happiness, what my family experience when I won the scholarship.
And I left for Vilna, and attended the Real-Gymnasium in Vilna.
It was a Yiddish Real-Gymnasium.
Vilna was a beautiful city in Poland.
Vilna was considered the cradle of Jewish literature.
In Vilna we had a Real-Gymnasium.
In Vilna you had the YIVO, the Yidisher Visnshaftlekher Institut.
Jewish Scientific Institute, where research work was going on the Jewish language.
In Vilna we had the-- the greatest library in Poland, Strashun Library.
In Vilna we had the Jewish theater and other institutions.
Vilna was a large town.
My town was a small, a little town.
Vilna was a large, large town.
My town was a little town, but it-- but it was in a very high intellectual level.
We had, in our town, a Yiddish school.
We had in our town a Hebrew school.
We had in our town a Polish school and a school for underprivileged children.
And my--
The population was 4,000-- 4,000 Jews lived in Zdzieciol.
Yeah.
The total population was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 5,000, over, something like that.
Primarily Jewish.
Not exactly, not exactly, because we were mainly concentrated in the center of town, and we were involved in the Jewish life and did not-- we weren't very much involved with the Gentile around us.
So we did not experience that much anti-Semitism.
My father- my father had a store where he was selling, like, for instance, sweaters and [POLISH] sweaters, stockings, and socks, and shirts, things like that.
And besides, he was also an insurance broker.
He insured people for fire, life insurance, and so on.
Yeah.
But my father was-- I would say he was a scholar.
He knew Hebrew real well.
He knew Yiddish.
He knew Polish.
And he was a very intelligent man.
My mother helped my father in the business, and my mother brought us up at home, she did things for us.
The shtetl.
--Jewish community life in this town.
I-- I would say the Jewish life in our town was in a very high intellectual level.
Mainly the Jewish-- the Jewish-- the Jews were involved in stores.
They had stores.
They-- they were involved-- part of the population in our town was involved in the-- in the Jewish school, and part of the population was involved in the Hebrew school.
And it was always a fight between the two sections.

About-- about the ideas, about the ideology, about bringing up the children, about the Yiddishists, the Jews that belonged to the-- to the Jewish school, they were aiming to improve life in Poland, to improve life in our shtetl.
The Zionists, the ones who belonged to the Hebrew school, they were aiming to go to Israel, to have our own land, our own country.
Maybe they were right.
So it was always a fight between the two groups.
But mainly, it was a nice, quiet town.
And I long for this little town, for all the people who lived there, for all the friends, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for my family, whom we lost during the Second World War.
Not a very extended family.
I had my-- my aunt, my uncle, who also lived in shtetl.
They were-- that was a sister of my father, and the name was Nachamke-- Nachamke.
And with her husband, [?
Hershel, ?] and their three children, three sons.
Unfortunately they all were killed during the Second World War.
Yes, my oldest cousin was Yeisef, his brother, Meishe, and his younger brother, Gavrielke.
And their last name, Benyaminovitch.
My mother's brother lived in Vilna.
He was married, and he lived in Vilna.
His name was Srober, and his wife's name was [?
Mary. ?] She was a dentist, and she had two children from her first husband, who was a veterinarian, and he died during the Second World War.
And she was married to my mother's brother.
And they had another little boy, Torinke.
Unfortunately, they're all gone.
So I long-- I long for my relatives.
I long for my good friends in my town.
In my town.
My aunt-- I always wanted to ride a bicycle, but I did not have a bicycle.
So my cousins, they had a bicycle.
And they allowed me to take the bicycle, and to take a ride.
I was so happy, I felt so good that I'm riding now the bicycle, and I shared it with my teacher.
And I remember he asked me, how many times did you-- did-- did you fall?
How many times?
I said, not even once.
He says, I don't think you know how to ride a bicycle.
So that I remember, I remember.
We had a poor life, but a joyful life.
Was it a religious family?
It wasn't that religious, but we would spend holidays together.
We celebrated together.
We enjoyed together, like, for instance, on Friday we would celebrate Shabbat.
My grandfather would work the whole day preparing the food for Shabbat, for Friday.
We had a gentile woman would come and clean the house, and then the whole family would sit around a table, and celebrate Shabbat.
And then on Saturday, Saturday morning, we would eat the cholent.
Cholent is prepared potatoes with meat, that we would prepare it and take it to the bakery, and leave it there overnight.
And then, Saturday morning, we would pick it up, bring it home, sit around the table, and enjoy the cholent.
Then we would take in nice walk, the whole family, my mother, father, my brother, and sister.
So that was something special.
It was something special to remember.
But it's over.
And it seems to be so far, like it would never, never exist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I went back to Zdzieciol after the war, and I did not expect to find much, but I found much less than I expected.
Zdzieciol looked like a little village.
Yes, I remember that I used-- I liked to skate.
So we had a river in our town.
In wintertime, it was frozen.
So I went skating on the river, and ice broke, and I fell into the water.
And one boy was going to rescue me, so he walked on the ice, and he also fell in.

Then they took us out, and I remember they took us to a neighborhood house, and they gave us some vodka to drink so nothing would happen to us because everything was frozen, everything what we had on was frozen.
And nothing happened to us, but it was quite an experience.
I-- I was at the time, I think about 12, 13 years old.
And I came to school, and I told the story to my teacher, and I told him that I fell into the water, and he called me, in Polish, [POLISH], that means that I fell in the water.
I was in the water-- wintertime, can you imagine, I mean, it's so cold-- I was in the water.
So this is also something to remember.
I'm glad you asked me, and I could tell you about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] You don't remember that it ever existed.
That was so long, long, long time ago.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So--
You're right.
My memory is that it was a beautiful town.
And my uncle lived in Vilna, as I mentioned before, so when I came to Vilna, I lived with them.
I attended the Real-Gymnasium.
I was about 15 years old, 14, 15.
I loved my gymnasium, it was something very special.
First of all, I liked to live in a big city, so after coming from my town, it was something very special for me.
I liked the comradeship, I liked being with my friends, I liked-- I made friends in Vilna.
I was a good student, I would say.
I studied a lot.
I studied-- all the subjects were thought in Yiddish, only geography, Polish history-- in Polish, in Polish.
But we had to take a foreign language.
I don't know why, but I chose English, not knowing that my-- it's going to be my children's mother tongue.
So I remember that our teacher, she had red hair, and she was from England.
And we called her, the yellow [? picture, ?] because she had red hair, because we liked her a lot, so that's the way we called her.
We had to take up Latin-- I-- I studied Latin-- we had to take up Hebrew, Yiddish, math, chemistry, physics, theology, all the subjects.
And [SIGHS] those were beautiful, wonderful years.
But when it came time to go back to my town, I used to count, first, the months, then the days, then the hours, when I'll go back and see my parents, and my brother and sister.
Those were beautifully years in Vilna, but it did not last long.
I was there for almost four years, and then the war interrupted us.
And I-- I went back to Zdzieciol, and that was during the summer.
I could not to return anymore because Vilna was given away to the Lithuanians, and it became the capital of Lithuania.
So I was afraid that if I go back to Vilna, I will never be able to return to my home.
That was the summer of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's see.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it was the summer of '41, if I'm not mistaken.
In Vilna, we had a beautiful life there.
First of all, we would go every month to the Jewish theater, see all kind of plays, all kind of performances.
I spent lots of time at the library, Strashun Bibliotheque, where I was reading Jewish books, studying-- it was nice and quiet.
It felt good.
I visited the YIVO, the Jewish Scientific Institute, where they showed us around, where we were doing some research, preparing some papers.
They recommended to us which books we can use, and YIVO was a beautiful, beautiful institution, organization.
And it was-- they were situated, I remember the street, on [?
Vivulska, ?] [?
Ulitsa ?] [?
Vivulska, ?] in-- in Vilna.
And it felt good to be there and to visit with different people.
I had friends.
We would go out to dances.
We had a special cafeteria in Vilna, I remember, where we used to meet.
And they called us [POLISH], that means, in Polish, students.
We would meet at this particular cafeteria, had meals there, danced, enjoyed it.
So that was something very special.
In Vilna, there was a beautiful main street, [POLISH], with all kinds of stores, all kind of beautiful offices.
We would go and visit, and walk around, and enjoy, and laugh.
And it was something very special.
My father would come to Vilna to buy some merchandise for the store that they had in Zdzieciol.
Every time, before he would have-- had to leave for Zdzieciol, I would cry so much.
I did not want to part with him.
It felt so good to have him around.
OK.
